summary: Jeff Atwood provides a practical roundup of techniques for managing ASP.NET viewstate, a notoriously problematic feature that bloats page sizes with Base64-encoded data. He covers four main strategies: disabling viewstate when possible, moving it to the bottom of pages for better SEO, compressing it for marginal size reduction, and storing it server-side. The post also highlights ASP.NET 2.0's improvements including ControlState separation and a new formatter that halves viewstate size. It's a concise, link-heavy resource post typical of mid-2000s technical blogging.
key_points: The first rule of viewstate is to turn it off whenever possible
Moving viewstate to page bottom improves Google indexing since Google only indexes the first N bytes
Compressing viewstate yields marginal benefits compared to plain text compression
Server-side viewstate storage (Session, Application, or Cache) is an alternative to client-side
ASP.NET 2.0 introduced ControlState and ObjectStateFormatter, significantly improving viewstate handling
money_quotes: The first rule of thumb, of course, is to turn it off whenever you can.
if you have 12 kb of Base64 encoded schmutz in your header, that isn't exactly helping people find your pages via Google searches
Here's to progress. Now pardon me while I crack my bullwhip.
key_insight: ASP.NET viewstate is a necessary evil that requires active management through disabling, relocating, compressing, or moving server-side to avoid SEO penalties and performance issues.
